title: Panzanella (Tuscan Bread Salad)

ingredients.name: stale country bread or ciabatta, torn into chunks

subs.name: toasted idli or leftover thick roti torn into pieces
subs.notes: roti makes a convincing panzanella; toast it lightly before adding so it holds up to soaking

subs.name: deseeded ridge gourd (turai) or thinly sliced capsicum
subs.notes: capsicum adds a pleasant crunch and sweetness

ingredients.name: red onion or white onion, thinly sliced

subs.name: fresh coriander and mint leaves
subs.notes: use half and half; both herbs bring freshness that echoes the role of basil

ingredients.name: red wine vinegar or white vinegar

subs.name: lemon juice or a splash of diluted tamarind water
subs.notes: tamarind gives an Indian-fusion tang that pairs beautifully with fresh basil

steps.text: Sprinkle a little water or dip the bread chunks very briefly in a bowl of cold water — 5–10 seconds only. Squeeze out firmly with both hands until the bread is damp but not dripping. Break into rough pieces and place in a large mixing bowl.
Add the tomatoes to the bowl and toss with a good pinch of salt. Let them sit for 5 minutes — the salt draws out their juices, which will season the bread and dress the salad naturally.
Add the cucumber, onion and basil to the bowl. Drizzle over the olive oil and vinegar. Season generously with salt and pepper.

Toss everything together gently and allow the salad to rest for 10 minutes at room temperature before serving — this is critical. The bread needs time to soak up the dressing and tomato juices and become cohesive.
Taste once more for salt, drizzle with a last thread of olive oil and serve at room temperature. Panzanella does not keep — eat it the same day.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bread → toasted idli or leftover thick roti torn into pieces (roti makes a convincing panzanella; toast it lightly before adding so it holds up to soaking); cucumber → deseeded ridge gourd (turai) or thinly sliced capsicum (capsicum adds a pleasant crunch and sweetness); vinegar → lemon juice or a splash of diluted tamarind water (tamarind gives an Indian-fusion tang that pairs beautifully with fresh basil); basil → fresh coriander and mint leaves (use half and half; both herbs bring freshness that echoes the role of basil)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Day-old bread from any local bakery works perfectly — the staler the better. Sourdough or multigrain bread from chains like Theobroma or Paul also works well. Regular white vinegar from any kirana store is an ideal substitute for red wine vinegar. Indian cucumbers tend to be more watery than European ones — deseed and salt them separately for 5 minutes and drain before adding to prevent a soggy salad.
